metadata.purpose: Assess prerequisite knowledge before learning to compare and order 5-digit numbers

prerequisiteConcepts.conceptName: Ordering numbers in ascending and descending order
Using place value to compare numbers

questions.question: Which symbol makes this statement true? 5,678 ___ 5,876
questions.explanation: Compare the hundreds place: 6 < 8, so 5,678 < 5,876. The first number is smaller.

commonMistakes.conceptGap: Understanding place value comparison from left to right
commonMistakes.remediation: Review comparing from highest place value

questions.question: Which number is greater: 7,234 or 7,189?
questions.explanation: Thousands are same (7). Compare hundreds: 2 > 1, so 7,234 > 7,189.

commonMistakes.remediation: Practice comparing from left to right

questions.question: Arrange in ascending order (smallest to largest): 3,456; 3,654; 3,465
questions.explanation: Compare hundreds: all have 4. Compare tens: 5, 6, 6. So 3,456 < 3,465 < 3,654.

commonMistakes.mistake: Arranged in descending instead of ascending

questions.question: Which list shows numbers in descending order (largest to smallest)?
questions.explanation: Descending means largest to smallest. 8,900 > 8,090 > 8,009 (compare hundreds: 9 > 0 > 0).

commonMistakes.mistake: Arranged in ascending instead of descending

questions.question: Fill in the missing number: 2,100; 2,300; ___; 2,700 (ascending order)
questions.explanation: The pattern increases by 200 each time. 2,300 + 200 = 2,500.

commonMistakes.remediation: Check the difference between consecutive numbers

questions.question: When comparing two 4-digit numbers, which place value should you check first?
questions.explanation: Always start comparing from the highest place value (left side). For 4-digit numbers, that's the thousands place.

questions.question: True or False: If the thousands digits are the same, you should compare the hundreds digits next.
questions.explanation: True! If digits in one place are equal, move to the next place value to the right to compare.

commonMistakes.mistake: Didn't understand the place-by-place comparison method

questions.question: What does the symbol '>' mean?
questions.explanation: The symbol '>' means 'greater than.' The open side faces the larger number. Example: 5 > 3.

commonMistakes.remediation: Remember: the open mouth faces the bigger number

questions.question: Which statement is true about the numbers 15,000 and 9,999?
questions.explanation: 15,000 is a 5-digit number and 9,999 is a 4-digit number. A 5-digit number is always greater than a 4-digit number. So 15,000 > 9,999.

commonMistakes.conceptGap: Understanding that digit count matters first
commonMistakes.remediation: Review that more digits = larger number

excellent.message: Excellent! You can compare and order 4-digit numbers well. You're ready to learn about comparing and ordering 5-digit numbers.

good.message: Good work! You understand comparison and ordering. Let's extend this to 5-digit numbers.

partial.message: You have some gaps in comparing or ordering numbers. Watch review videos before learning 5-digit comparison and ordering.

needs_review.message: You need to review how to compare and order 4-digit numbers first. We'll start with basics, then move to 5-digit numbers.

feedbackMessages.on_completion: Great job completing the readiness assessment!
feedbackMessages.low_score_encouragement: Don't worry! We'll show you videos to help you learn to compare and order numbers correctly.
feedbackMessages.high_score_praise: Fantastic! You're well-prepared to learn about comparing and ordering 5-digit numbers.
feedbackMessages.retake_option: After watching the recommended videos, you can retake this assessment to check your progress.
